symptoms.symptom_name: Continuous RUQ pain persisting beyond 8 hours
symptoms.onset: after a fatty meal ~8 hours ago

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Duration beyond 6 hours distinguishes established cholecystitis from transient biliary colic.
Biliary colic is transient (<6 hours); sustained pain argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: RUQ pain radiating to the right scapula
symptoms.character: boring through to the right shoulder blade

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Referred pain to the right scapula/shoulder tip is characteristic of gallbladder pathology.
Systemic inflammation supports established cholecystitis over simple colic.
Fever plus RUQ pain raises cholangitis — screen for jaundice, rigors and confusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Repeated vomiting with inability to keep fluids down

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nausea and vomiting commonly accompany acute cholecystitis; inability to hydrate adds dehydration risk and supports hospital care.
Fatty-meal-triggered gallbladder contraction against an obstructing stone is a classic precipitant.
Pre-existing cholelithiasis is the substrate for calculous cholecystitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on subcostal palpation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Murphy's sign is a highly specific bedside indicator of gallbladder inflammation — sealed clinician finding, not elicitable via telehealth.
Localised peritonism confirms established inflammation; requires clinician palpation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (HR 105) and measured fever (38.4) with stable BP (135/85) and SpO2 98%

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Systemic inflammatory response with preserved blood pressure and oxygenation — no shock; sealed clinician measurement (the AI infers systemic upset from patient-reported fever/vomiting, not a measured HR/BP).

symptoms.symptom_name: No jaundice, dark urine or pale stools

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of obstructive jaundice makes common-bile-duct obstruction and cholangitis less likely, though fever keeps it screened.

symptoms.symptom_name: Thickened gallbladder wall on abdominal ultrasound

investigation_details.expected_result: Thickened gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid and gallstones (anticipated on formal imaging)
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Diagnosis unconfirmed and severity ungraded; missed complications (empyema, gangrene, perforation).

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Ultrasound wall thickening with pericholecystic fluid and a sonographic Murphy's sign confirms acute cholecystitis — hospital investigation, never available via telehealth in real time.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sharp inspiratory catch of the pain on deep breathing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A patient-reportable inspiratory catch reflects diaphragmatic movement against the inflamed gallbladder — a lay proxy for the clinician-elicited Murphy's sign.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Continuous RUQ pain >6 hours + fever + positive Murphy's sign together indicate established acute cholecystitis rather than transient biliary colic.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line imaging to confirm acute cholecystitis (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, stones) — arranged via ED, not obtainable in telehealth.
Quantify the systemic inflammatory response and support severity grading.
Screen for biliary obstruction / cholangitis (raised bilirubin, ALP, GGT).
Exclude gallstone pancreatitis, which can mimic or complicate the presentation.
If systemically septic, to guide antibiotics before hospital treatment.
